began on April 15, 1952. The regular season ended on September 28, with the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The postseason began with Game 1 of the [49th World Series](/wiki/1952_World_Series "1952 World Series") on October 1 and ended with Game 7 on October 7. In the fourth iteration of this [Subway Series](/wiki/Subway_Series "Subway Series") World Series matchup, the Yankees defeated the Dodgers, four games to three, capturing their 15th championship in franchise history, and their fourth in a five-run World Series. This was the fourth World Series between the two teams.

The **1952 major league baseball season** began on April 15, 1952. The regular season ended on September 28, with the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The postseason began with Game 1 of the [49th World Series](/wiki/1952_World_Series "1952 World Series") on October 1 and ended with Game 7 on October 7. In the fourth iteration of this [Subway Series](/wiki/Subway_Series "Subway Series") World Series matchup, the Yankees defeated the Dodgers, four games to three, capturing their 15th championship in franchise history, and their fourth in a five-run World Series. This was the fourth World Series between the two teams.

The [19th Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/1952_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1952 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") was held on July 8 at [Shibe Park](/wiki/Shibe_Park "Shibe Park") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), hosted by the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies"). The [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") won, 3–2. To date, it was the only All-Star Game to be called early due to rain.

The 1952 season would prove to be the final season of a 50-season run which saw no team relocate from one city to another, as the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves") would move to [Milwaukee, Wisconsin](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") the [following year](/wiki/1953_Major_League_Baseball_season "1953 Major League Baseball season") as the [Milwaukee Braves](/wiki/Milwaukee_Braves "Milwaukee Braves"). 1952 would also be [Ford Frick](/wiki/Ford_Frick "Ford Frick")'s first full year as commissioner.

## Schedule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1952_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Schedule")]

See also: [Major League Baseball schedule](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_schedule "Major League Baseball schedule")

The 1952 schedule consisted of 154 games for all teams in the American League and National League, each of which had eight teams. Each team was scheduled to play 22 games against the other seven teams of their respective league. This continued the format put in place since the [1904](/wiki/1904_Major_League_Baseball_season "1904 Major League Baseball season") season (except for [1919](/wiki/1919_Major_League_Baseball_season "1919 Major League Baseball season")) and would be used until [1961](/wiki/1961_Major_League_Baseball_season "1961 Major League Baseball season") in the American League and [1962](/wiki/1962_Major_League_Baseball_season "1962 Major League Baseball season") in the National League.

Opening Day took place on April 15, featuring 12 teams. The final day of the scheduled regular season was on September 28, which saw all sixteen teams play, continuing the trend from [1946](/wiki/1946_Major_League_Baseball_season "1946 Major League Baseball season"). The [World Series](/wiki/1952_World_Series "1952 World Series") took place between October 1 and October 7.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1952_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The 1952 season saw the following rule changes:

* The [Open Classification](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball#Postwar_changes "Minor League Baseball") level was created specifically for the [Pacific Coast League](/wiki/Pacific_Coast_League "Pacific Coast League") (PCL), a level of play between [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") and [major-league](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (this level of play would be eliminated following the PCL's reversion to Triple-A in [1958](/wiki/1958_in_baseball "1958 in baseball")). Unlike other levels of the minor league, having an Open Classification came with the following stipulations:[[1]](#cite_note-1951_Winter_SABR-1)
  + Players at the Open Classification would be excluded from the draft if they waived their selection rights or had less than five years of service.
  + The draft price for an Open Classification player would be $15,000 (equivalent to $182,000 in 2025) instead of the $10,000 (equivalent to $121,000 in 2025) price tag on Triple-A players.
  + To achieve Open Classification, a league had to show an aggregate population of 10 million, have an aggregate park capacity of 120,000, and average a paid attendance of 2,250,000 for the preceding five years.
* An 11-step process was established for a league to reach major-league status:[[1]](#cite_note-1951_Winter_SABR-1)
  1. Any group of eight teams mutually agreeing to all requirements and responsibilities as provided under the proposed regulations must apply to the major-league Executive Council. The eight teams are responsible for all necessary territorial indemnities and financial obligations.
  2. The teams must present with their application complete data establishing their ability to meet the requirements for advanced status, including a full statement of stock ownership, financial ability, and character, both for the group and its individual members.
  3. The proposed league shall show an aggregate population of 15 million in the eight cities.
  4. Each team shall have a potential capacity of at least 25,000 in its ballpark.
  5. They shall have had an average total paid attendance of 3.5 million over a three-year period preceding the application.
  6. They shall provide a balanced schedule of at least 154 games.
  7. They shall adopt the major-league minimum-salary agreement with no maximum salary limitations.
  8. They will become parties to the Major League Agreement and the Major-Minor League Agreement.
  9. They will accept the uniform major-league player’s contract and agreement.
  10. They will join in the players’ [pension plan](/wiki/Pension "Pension") or adopt a comparable plan.
  11. They shall apply for major-league status at least six months before the meeting at which the application must be considered, and at least 10 months before the opening of the season in which they hope to participate under major-league status.
* A new rule regarding high-school players was approved. Players could be contacted at any time, but could not sign until their high-school eligibility expired. Any student who dropped out of high school before their eligibility expired must sit out one year before being eligible to sign.[[1]](#cite_note-1951_Winter_SABR-1) Previously, the rules regarding high-school players was that a player could not be signed until they had received their degree or their class graduated was eliminated.[[2]](#cite_note-1950_Winter_SABR-2)
* Major-league players now have the right to have a liaison in the commissioner's office. The liaison would be a full-time representative and serve as a clearinghouse for any player problems that might arise.[[1]](#cite_note-1951_Winter_SABR-1)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1952_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Venues")]

The [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves") would play their last game at [Braves Field](/wiki/Braves_Field "Braves Field") on September 21 against the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers"), relocating to [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") at [Milwaukee County Stadium](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Stadium "Milwaukee County Stadium") as the [Milwaukee Braves](/wiki/Milwaukee_Braves "Milwaukee Braves") for the start of the [1953](/wiki/1953_Major_League_Baseball_season "1953 Major League Baseball season") season.

## Retired numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1952_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Retired numbers")]

* [Honus Wagner](/wiki/Honus_Wagner "Honus Wagner") had his No. 33 retired by the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") on February 16. This was the first number retired by the team.
* [Joe DiMaggio](/wiki/Joe_DiMaggio "Joe DiMaggio") had his No. 5 retired by the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") on April 18. This was the third number retired by the team.
